contest.title: 2024 ICPC Asia Taichung Regional Contest (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Preferably Teams)

sources.notes: Read all four operations, the complete priority order, both samples, printable-ASCII rule, and limits n <= 1e6 and total pattern/append characters <= 2e6.

Read and visually checked page 6 of the official contest analysis. It proposes a trie whose nodes maintain the best active descendant, ancestor propagation on add/delete, and a cursor for append/backspace, while explicitly warning about null cursors, root backspaces, removed candidates, and child-RMQ implementation choices.

Actually read the independent Luogu write-up. It prebuilds the trie offline, identifies prefix matching with ancestry, depth with candidate length, ordered DFS rank with lexicographic order, and parent jumps with backspace; it also notes sparse maps for the 94-character alphabet.

Completed the Luogu reduction with terminal-ID sets and a segment tree over Euler positions, proved the comparator, and compared it against a naive active-pattern scan on 2,000 randomized valid operation streams with no mismatch.

annotation.summary: Maintain a dynamic set of ID-labeled patterns and a text t under append/backspace; after every operation return the active pattern prefixed by t with maximum length, then minimum lexicographic value, then minimum ID.
annotation.constraints: There are up to 1e6 operations and at most 2e6 total characters across added patterns and appended strings. Characters are printable non-space ASCII 33..126, IDs lie in [0,n], every added ID is unique, and every deletion is valid.
annotation.core_idea: Read all operations first and insert every pattern that will ever be added into one static trie. Traverse children in ASCII order and record depth plus Euler interval [tin[u],tout[u]] for every node. If the current text corresponds to node v, exactly the terminal nodes in v's subtree are matching patterns. At a terminal u, maintain the set of active IDs for that exact string and expose only its minimum ID. Give that Euler position the key (-depth[u], tin[u], minID): minimum key means longest string, then lexicographically smallest string because ordered-trie DFS follows lexicographic order, then smallest ID. A segment tree over Euler positions supports a point refresh on add/delete and a range-minimum query on [tin[v],tout[v]] after every action. Maintain a stack of trie states for all prefixes of t: append follows child edges (remaining null once a prefix is absent), while backspace pops at most to the root. Because all future pattern paths were prebuilt, a null state cannot become a valid path later during replay.
annotation.complexity: Let S <= 2e6 be total input characters and V <= S+1 the trie size. The offline Euler/segment-tree variant takes O(S log 94 + n log V) time with ordered maps, or O(S + n log V) with constant-alphabet transitions, and O(S+n) memory.
tricks: Prebuild all future pattern paths before replay so later add operations cannot invalidate a previously stored null cursor state.
Turn the prefix predicate into one trie-subtree interval.
Use ASCII-ordered DFS rank as the lexicographic tie-break among equal-length patterns.
At each terminal keep only the minimum active ID, then combine terminals with key (-depth,tin,id).
Store the trie state after every text prefix so total append and successful backspace movement is linear in appended characters.
Treat a null cursor and overlong backspace explicitly; never physically delete trie nodes.
uncertainty_points: No unresolved correctness uncertainty remains for the offline Euler/segment-tree variant after randomized differential testing.
The official per-node propagation variant is retained with medium confidence because the PDF intentionally leaves the child-RMQ container and static-universe preprocessing to implementation.
source_gaps: The official PDF is high level and does not fully specify how to prebuild paths or update every ancestor efficiently.

The Luogu prose supplies the offline trie/DFS reduction but does not name the final subtree range-query structure; its linked Codeforces submission was not directly accessible, so the segment-tree completion is explicitly self-derived.

solution_variants.name: offline-trie-euler-segment-tree
solution_variants.summary: Prebuild all pattern paths, flatten the trie in ASCII DFS order, store each terminal's best active ID at its Euler point, and range-query the current prefix node's subtree with a segment tree under the exact priority comparator.
solution_variants.complexity: O(S log 94 + n log V) time and O(S+n) memory, with V <= S+1.

solution_variants.name: per-node-best-propagation
solution_variants.summary: Prebuild the trie, keep every node's best active terminal in its subtree, and on terminal add/delete propagate changed child-best keys through ancestors using a small-alphabet scan or per-node ordered container; append/backspace moves a cursor whose node directly stores the answer.
solution_variants.complexity: O(S log 94 + n) total time with per-node ordered child-best containers and summed add/delete path lengths O(S), plus O(S+n) memory.

tags.evidence: The trie makes the current text a node and all matching patterns exactly that node's descendant terminals, while append and backspace become child and parent movements.

Dynamic add/delete operations refresh one terminal's Euler point, and after every action a segment-tree range minimum over the current node's subtree returns the best active candidate under (-depth,tin,id).
ASCII-ordered DFS flattens every prefix node's descendant set into [tin,tout], converting the tree-subtree candidate query into one contiguous range query.
All operations are read once before replay so every future pattern path can be inserted and Euler-numbered in a static trie; active updates and outputs are then replayed in original order.
Visiting trie children by ASCII value makes terminal Euler order equal string lexicographic order, supplying the second candidate tie-break without comparing full strings.
The solution needs sparse 94-character transitions, terminal ID sets, null-state cursor handling, parent stacks, and fast I/O, but these implement the trie-subtree reduction.
